items.title: What UAT covers vs what it omits
items.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Continuous target f* on compact domain] --> B[Wide MLP + non-linear activation]
  B --> C[Can approximate f* arbitrarily well]
  C --> D{Also guaranteed?}
  D -->|No| E[Generalization on unseen data]
  D -->|No| F[Finding weights by SGD]
  D -->|No| G[Small network / data efficiency]
  D -->|No| H[Sample complexity bounds]
items.caption: UAT is an existence/representation result (left path). Generalization, optimization, and efficiency (right) require separate theory and practice.

items.title: Width vs depth in feedforward MLPs
items.mermaid:
flowchart TB
  shallowTitle["Classic UAT: one wide hidden layer"]
  shallowTitle --> I1[Input]
  I1 --> H1[Hidden width W large]
  H1 --> O1[Output]
  deepTitle["Depth alternative: stacked hidden layers"]
  deepTitle --> I2[Input]
  I2 --> H2a[Hidden 1]
  H2a --> H2b[Hidden 2]
  H2b --> H2c[Hidden 3]
  H2c --> O2[Output]
items.caption: Classic UAT: one sufficiently wide hidden layer suffices for approximation. Deep stacks may achieve similar expressivity with fewer total units for some functions.

items.title: From expressivity to deployment checklist
items.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  UAT[UAT: expressive hypothesis class exists] --> Arch[Pick width/depth + activation]
  Arch --> Train[Train with SGD/Adam]
  Train --> Val[Measure validation error]
  Val --> Gen{Val error acceptable?}
  Gen -->|Yes| Deploy[Deploy with monitoring]
  Gen -->|No| Fix[Regularize / more data / tune architecture]
  Fix --> Train
items.caption: UAT motivates step one; validation-driven iteration handles what the theorem does not cover.
